revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Prince (son of Emperor)]][[ja:皇子]][[ko:황자]][[vi:Hoàng tử]][[zh-min-nan:Hông-chú]][[zh:皇子]]
{{Unified Characters|櫛}}
'''Prince''' (皇子) (みこ、おうじ) is the son of an {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|qid=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}} or {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Tennō|1=Emperor of Japan}}, that is, the child of an emperor. In a broader sense the term can refer to children of either gender,<ref>See Article {{ill|Imperial Household Law|en|Imperial Household Law|qid=Q1324733|lt=Imperial Household Law|1=Imperial Household Law}} Article 6 for details</ref> though such usage is rare. The daughters of an emperor or tennō are called '''{{ill|Princess|en|Princess|qid=Q863048|lt=Princess|1=Princess}}''' (おうじょ). In some countries, titles such as “{{ill|Imperial Prince|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Prince|ja|親王|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Prince|lt_ja=親王号|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince|qq=}}”, “{{ill|Duke|ja|郡王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Duke|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”, “{{ill|King|en|King|qid=Q12097|lt=King|1=King}}”, or “{{ill|Grand duke|en|Grand duke|qid=Q205706|lt=Grand Duke|1=Grand duke}}” are conferred, with the title “{{ill|King|en|King|qid=Q12097|lt=King|1=King}}” representing a Rank immediately subordinate to that of the emperor.
== Japan == Originally, during the era of the {{ill|Yamato Kingship|en|Yamato Kingship|qid=Q2658252|lt=Yamato Government|1=Yamato Kingship}}, the term “miko” (御子) was widely used to refer to the children of powerful {{ill|Gōzoku|en|Gōzoku|qid=Q84066|lt=clans|1=Gōzoku}}. However, over time it came to denote specifically the offspring of the {{ill|Okimi|en|Okimi|qid=Q718995|lt=Great King|1=Okimi}}, and with the establishment of the {{ill|Tennō|ja|天皇号|lt=Tennō Title|lt_ja=天皇号|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tennō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天皇|1=Emperor of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q208233}}, the characters for “kōshi” (皇子) began to be used. According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, one of the reasons for the execution of {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|qid=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} during the {{ill|Taika Reform|en|Taika Reform|qid=Q570140|lt=Taika Reform|1=Taika Reform}} was his audacious act of having his own children referred to as “miko.”
With the advent of the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, and the subsequent establishment of specific court Ranks for the emperor’s offspring—such as the titles {{ill|Imperial Prince|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Prince|ja|親王|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Prince|lt_ja=親王号|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince|qq=}} and {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|qid=Q863048|lt=Princess|1=Princess}}—the generic usage of “kōshi” and “kōjo” to designate imperial children fell out of practice.
In modern Japan, usage of the term is still attested in the {{ill|Imperial Household Law|en|Imperial Household Law|qid=Q1324733|lt=Imperial Household Law|1=Imperial Household Law}}, where expressions such as “First Prince”, “Second Prince”, etc., appear in Article 2; and in Article 8 the crown prince (the designated heir) is referred to as the {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|qid=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}}. Moreover, terms such as “First Imperial Son”, “Second Imperial Son”, “First Imperial Daughter”, etc., are employed to indicate the relation to the emperor (for example, if the current emperor does not have a son, he may be designated as “○○ Tennō Dai-ichi Kōshi” – in the case of the retired emperor {{ill|Akihito|en|Akihito|qid=Q37979|lt=Akihito|1=Akihito}}, he is referred to as “{{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|qid=Q34479|lt=Shōwa Tennō|1=Hirohito}} Dai-ichi Kōshi”). Additionally, within the Imperial Palace the rooms of the emperor’s children are referred to as the “Prince’s Chamber,” among other uses of the term.
== China == {{Stub|This section is a stub}} In China, the first emperor of the {{ill|Qin (state)|en|Qin (state)|qid=Q34756|lt=Qin dynasty|1=Qin (state)}}, {{ill|Qin Shi Huang|en|Qin Shi Huang|qid=Q7192|lt=Shi Huangdi|1=Qin Shi Huang}}, designated the sons born of his consorts as princes.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=【驚愕】最新技術で頭蓋骨から顔を復元！明らかになった秦の始皇帝にまつわる事実とは？｜TBSテレビ|url=https://topics.tbs.co.jp/article/detail/?id=2002|website=TBS Topics|access-date=2023-03-10|language=ja}}</ref> The term “prince” originally means “the son of the {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|qid=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}}.” In subsequent Chinese dynasties the male offspring of the emperor continued to be called princes; however, following the abolition of the imperial system after the fall of the {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|qid=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}}, the title fell into disuse.
== Footnotes == {{Notes Help}} {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial Prince|lt=Imperial Prince|lt=Prince|ja|親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince|qq=}} * {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|qid=Q863048|lt=Princess|1=Princess}} * {{ill|Princess|en|Princess|qid=Q863048|lt=Princess|1=Princess}} * {{ill|Crown prince|en|Crown prince|qid=Q207293|lt=Crown Prince|1=Crown prince}}
{{translated page|ja|皇子|version=104426101|comment=Imported full ja history}}
